paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## IMPROVED DENOISING DIFFUSION PROBABILISTIC MODELS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

We explore denoising diffusion probabilistic models, a class of generative models which have recently been shown to produce excellent samples in the image and audio domains. While these models produce excellent samples, it has yet to be shown that they can achieve competitive log-likelihoods. We show that, with several small modifications, diffusion models can achieve competitive log-likelihoods in the image domain while maintaining high sample quality. Additionally, our models allow for sampling with an order of magnitude fewer diffusion steps with only a modest difference in sample quality. Finally, we explore how sample quality and log-likelihood scale with the number of diffusion steps and the amount of model capacity. We conclude that denoising diffusion probabilistic models are a promising class of generative models with excellent scaling properties and sample quality.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Sohl-Dickstein et al. (2015) introduced diffusion probabilistic models ("diffusion models" for brevity), a class of generative models which match a data distribution by learning to reverse a gradual, multi-step noising process. More recently, Ho et al. (2020) showed an equivalence between these models and score based generative models (Song & Ermon, 2019; 2020), which learn a gradient of the log-density of the data distribution using denoising score matching (Hyvärinen, 2005). It has recently been shown that this class of models can produce high-quality images (Ho et al., 2020; Song & Ermon, 2020; Jolicoeur-Martineau et al., 2020) and audio (Chen et al., 2020b; Kong et al., 2020), but it has yet to be shown that diffusion models can achieve competitive log-likelihoods. Furthermore, while Ho et al. (2020) showed extremely good results on the CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky, 2009) and LSUN (Yu et al., 2015) datasets, it is unclear how well diffusion models scale to datasets with higher diversity such as ImageNet. Finally, while Chen et al. (2020b) found that diffusion models can efficiently generate audio using a small number of sampling steps, it has yet to be shown that the same is true for images.

In this paper, we show that diffusion models can achieve competitive log-likelihoods while maintaining good sample quality, even on high-diversity datasets like ImageNet. Additionally, we show that our improved models can produce competitive samples an order of magnitude faster than those from Ho et al. (2020). We achieve these results by combining a simple reparameterization of the reverse process variance, a hybrid learning objective that combines the variational lower-bound with the simplified objective from Ho et al. (2020), and a novel noise schedule which allows the model to better leverage the entire diffusion process.

We find surprisingly that, with our hybrid objective, our models obtain better log-likelihoods than those obtained by optimizing the log-likelihood directly, and discover that the latter objective has much more gradient noise during training. We show that a simple importance sampling technique reduces this noise and allows us to achieve better log-likelihoods than with the hybrid objective. Using our trained models, we study how sample quality and log-likelihood change as we adjust the number of diffusion steps used at sampling time. We demonstrate that our improved models allow us to use an order of magnitude fewer steps at test time with only a modest change in sample quality and log-likelihood, thus speeding up sampling for use in practical applications.
Finally, we evaluate the performance of these models as we increase model size, and observe trends that suggest predictable improvements in performance as we increase training compute.

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## 2 DENOISING DIFFUSION PROBABILISTIC MODELS

We briefly review the formulation of diffusion models from Ho et al. (2020). This formulation makes various simplifying assumptions, such as a fixed noising process _q_ which adds diagonal Gaussian noise at each timestep. For a more general derivation, see Sohl-Dickstein et al. (2015).

## 2.1 DEFINITIONS

Given a data distribution _x_ 0 _∼ q_ ( _x_ 0), we define a forward noising process _q_ which produces latents _x_ 1 through _xT_ by adding Gaussian noise at time _t_ with variance _βt ∈_ (0 _,_ 1) as follows:

Given sufficiently large _T_ and a well behaved schedule of _βt_ , the latent _xT_ is nearly an isotropic Gaussian distribution. Thus, if we know the exact reverse distribution _q_ ( _xt−_ 1 _|xt_ ), we can sample _xT ∼N_ (0 _,_ **I** ) and run the process in reverse to get a sample from _q_ ( _x_ 0). However, since _q_ ( _xt−_ 1 _|xt_ ) depends on the entire data distribution, we approximate it using a neural network:
The combination of _q_ and _p_ is a variational auto-encoder (Kingma & Welling, 2013), and we can write the variational lower bound (VLB) as follows:

Aside from _L_ 0, each term of Equation 4 is a _KL_ divergence between two Gaussian distributions, and can thus be evaluated in closed form. To evaluate _L_ 0 for images, we assume that each color component is divided into 256 bins, and we compute the probability of _pθ_ ( _x_ 0 _|x_ 1) landing in the correct bin (which is tractable using the CDF of the Gaussian distribution). Also note that while _LT_ does not depend on _θ_ , it will be close to zero if the forward noising process adequately destroys the data distribution so that _q_ ( _xT |x_ 0) _≈N_ (0 _,_ **I** ).
It is useful to define and derive several other quantities which are relevant to the forward noising process, so we repeat them here from Ho et al. (2020):

## 2.2 TRAINING IN PRACTICE

Equation 12 provides an efficient way to jump directly to an arbitrary step of the forward noising process. This makes it possible to randomly sample _t_ during training. Ho et al. (2020) uniformly sample _t_ for each image in each mini-batch.

There are many different ways to parameterize _µθ_ ( _xt, t_ ). The most obvious option is to predict _µ_ could then be fed through _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) directly with a neural network; alternatively, the network could predict _µ_ ˜( _xt, x_ 0); finally, the network could predict the noise _x ϵ_ added to0, and this output _x_ 0, and this noise could be used to predict _x_ 0 via
Ho et al. (2020) found that predicting _ϵ_ worked best, especially when combined with a reweighted loss function:

This objective can be seen as a reweighted form of _L_ vlb (without the terms affecting Σ _θ_ ). The authors found that optimizing this reweighted objective resulted in much better sample quality than optimizing _L_ vlb directly, and explain this by drawing a connection to generative score matching (Song & Ermon, 2019; 2020).

One subtlety is that _L_ simple provides no learning signal for Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ). This is irrelevant, however, since Ho et al. (2020) achieved their best results by fixing the variance to _σt_[2] **[I]**[ rather than learning] it. They found that they achieve similar sample quality using either _σt_[2][=] _[β][t]_[or] _[ σ] t_[2][=] _[β]_[˜] _[t]_[, which are] two extremes given by _q_ ( _x_ 0) being either isotropic Gaussian noise or a delta function, respectively.

## 3 IMPROVING THE LOG-LIKELIHOOD

While Ho et al. (2020) found that diffusion models can generate high-fidelity samples according to FID (Heusel et al., 2017) and Inception Score (Salimans et al., 2016), they were unable to achieve competitive log-likelihoods with these models. Log-likelihood is a widely used metric in generative modeling, and it is generally believed that optimizing log-likelihood forces generative models to capture all of the modes of the data distribution (Razavi et al., 2019). Additionally, recent work (Henighan et al., 2020) has shown that small improvements in log-likelihood can have a dramatic impact on sample quality and learnt feature representations. Thus, it is important to explore why diffusion models seem to perform poorly on this metric, since this may suggest a fundamental shortcoming such as bad mode coverage. This section explores several modifications to the algorithm described in Section 2 that, when combined, allow diffusion models to achieve much better log-likelihoods on image datasets, suggesting that these models enjoy the same benefits as other likelihood-based generative models.

To study the effects of different modifications, we train fixed model architectures with fixed hyperparameters (Appendix A) on the ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 (van den Oord et al., 2016a) and CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky, 2009) datasets. While CIFAR-10 has seen more usage for this class of models, we chose to study ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 as well because it provides a good trade-off between diversity and resolution, allowing us to train models quickly without worrying about overfitting. Additionally, ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 has been studied extensively in the context of generative modeling (van den Oord et al., 2016b; Menick & Kalchbrenner, 2018; Child et al., 2019; Roy et al., 2020), allowing us to compare diffusion models directly to many other generative models.

The setup from Ho et al. (2020) (optimizing _L_ simple while setting _σt_[2][=] _[β][t]_[and] _[ T]_[=][1000][) achieves] a log-likelihood of **3.99 bits/dim** on ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 after 200K training iterations. We found in early experiments that we could get a boost in log-likelihood by increasing _T_ from 1000 to 4000; with this change, the log-likelihood improves to **3.77 bits/dim** . For the remainder of this section, we use _T_ = 4000, but we explore this choice in Section 4.

## 3.1 LEARNING Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ )

In Ho et al. (2020), the authors set Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) = _σt_[2] **[I]**[, where] _[ σ][t]_[is not learned.][Oddly, they found that] fixing˜ _σt_[2][to] _[ β][t]_[yielded roughly the same sample quality as fixing it to] _[β]_[˜] _[t]_[.][Considering that] _[ β][t]_[and] _βt_ represent two opposite extremes, it is reasonable to ask why this choice doesn’t affect samples. One clue is given by Figure 1a, which shows that _βt_ and _β_[˜] _t_ are almost equal except near _t_ = 0, i.e. where the model is dealing with imperceptible details. Furthermore, as we increase the number of diffusion steps, _βt_ and _β_[˜] _t_ seem to remain close to one another for more of the diffusion process.

This suggests that, in the limit of infinite diffusion steps, the choice of _σt_ might not matter at all for sample quality. In other words, as we add more diffusion steps, the model mean _µθ_ ( _xt, t_ ) determines the distribution much more than Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ).

While the above argument suggests that fixing _σt_ is a reasonable choice for the sake of sample quality, it says nothing about log-likelihood. In fact, Figure 1b shows that the first few steps of the diffusion process contribute the most to the variational lower bound. Thus, it seems likely that we could improve log-likelihood by using a better choice of Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ). To achieve this, we must learn Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) without the instabilities encountered by Ho et al. (2020).

Since Figure 1a shows that the reasonable range for Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) is very small, it would be hard for a neural network to predict Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) directly, even in the log domain, as observed by Ho et al. (2020). Instead, we found it better to parameterize the variance as an interpolation between _βt_ and _β_[˜] _t_ in the log domain. In particular, our model outputs a vector _v_ containing one component per dimension, and we turn this output into variances as follows:

We did not apply any constraints on _v_ , theoretically allowing the model to predict variances outside of the interpolated range. However, we did not observe the network doing this in practice, suggesting that the bounds for Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) are indeed expressive enough.

Since _L_ simple doesn’t depend on Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ), we define a new hybrid objective:

For our experiments, we set _λ_ = 0 _._ 001 to prevent _L_ vlb from overwhelming _L_ simple. Along this same line of reasoning, we also apply a stop-gradient to the _µθ_ ( _xt, t_ ) output for the _L_ vlb term. This way, _L_ vlb can guide Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) while _L_ simple is still the main source of influence over _µθ_ ( _xt, t_ ).

## 3.2 IMPROVING THE NOISE SCHEDULE

We found that the noise schedule used in Ho et al. (2020) was sub-optimal for ImageNet 64 _×_ 64. In particular, the end of the forward noising process is too noisy, and so doesn’t contribute very

To address this problem, we construct a different noise schedule in terms of _α_ ¯ _t_ :

To go from this definition to variances _βt_ , we note that _βt_ = 1 _− α_ ¯ _αt_ ¯ _−t_ 1[.][In practice, we clip] _[ β][t]_[to be] no larger than 0.999 to prevent singularities at the end of the diffusion process near _t_ = _T_ .

Our cosine schedule is designed to have a linear drop-off of _α_ ¯ _t_ in the middle of the process, while changing very little near the extremes of _t_ = 0 and _t_ = _T_ to prevent abrupt changes in noise level. Figure 3b shows how _α_ ¯ _t_ progresses for both schedules. We can see that the linear schedule from Ho et al. (2020) falls towards zero much faster, destroying information more quickly than necessary.

The small offset _s_ in our schedule prevents _βt_ from being too small near _t_ = 0, since we found that having tiny amounts of noise at the beginning of the process made it hard for the network to predict _ϵ_ accurately enough. In particular, we selected _s_ such that _[√] β_ 0 was slightly smaller than the pixel bin size, 1 _/_ 127 _._ 5. We chose to use _cos_[2] in particular because it is a common mathematical function with the shape we were looking for. This choice was arbitrary, and we expect that many other functions with similar shapes would work as well.

## 3.3 REDUCING GRADIENT NOISE

We expected to achieve the best log-likelihoods by optimizing _L_ vlb directly, rather than by optimizing _L_ hybrid. However, we were surprised to find that _L_ vlb was actually quite difficult to optimize in practice, at least on the diverse ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 dataset. Figure 4a shows the learning curves for both _L_ vlb and _L_ hybrid. Both curves are noisy, but the hybrid objective clearly achieves better log-likelihoods on the training set given the same amount of training time.

We hypothesized that the gradient of _L_ vlb was much noisier than that of _L_ hybrid. We confirmed this by evaluating the gradient noise scales (McCandlish et al., 2018) for models trained with both objectives, as shown in Figure 4b. Thus, we sought out a way to reduce the variance of _L_ vlb in order to optimize directly for log-likelihood.

Noting that different terms of _L_ vlb have greatly different magnitudes (Figure 1b), we hypothesized that sampling _t_ uniformly causes unnecessary noise in the _L_ vlb objective. To address this, we employ importance sampling:

Since _E_ [ _L_[2] _t_[]][is][unknown][beforehand][and][may][change][throughout][training,][we][maintain][a][history] of the previous 10 values for each loss term, and update this dynamically during training. At the beginning of training, we sample _t_ uniformly until we draw 10 samples for every _t ∈_ [0 _, T −_ 1]. With this importance sampled objective, we are able to achieve our best log-likelihoods by optimizing _L_ vlb.[1] This can be seen in Figure 4a as the " _Lvlb_ (resampled)" curve. The figure also shows that the importance sampled objective is considerably less noisy than the original, uniformly sampled objective.

## 3.4 RESULTS AND ABLATIONS

In this section, we ablate the changes we have made to achieve better log-likelihoods. Table 1 summarizes the results of our ablations on ImageNet 64 _×_ 64, and Table 2 shows them for CIFAR10. We also trained our best ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 models for 1.5M iterations, and report these results as well. Based on the results, we recommend always using the cosine schedule, and the _L_ hybrid objective in most cases. If one is only optimizing for likelihood and not sample quality, the importance sampled _L_ vlb is the best objective to use.
> 1We found that the importance sampling technique was not helpful when optimizing _L_ hybrid directly.

## 4 IMPROVING SAMPLING SPEED

All of our models were trained with 4000 diffusion steps, and thus producing a single sample takes several minutes on a modern GPU. In this section, we explore how performance scales if we reduce the steps used during sampling, and find that our pre-trained models can produce high-quality samples with many fewer diffusion steps than they were trained with without any fine-tuning. Reducing the steps in this way makes it possible to sample from our models in a number of seconds rather than minutes, and greatly improves the practical applicability of image diffusion models.

For a model trained with _T_ diffusion steps, we would typically sample using the same set of _t_ values (1 _,_ 2 _, ..., T_ ) as used during training. However, it is also possible to sample using an arbitrary set of _t_ values. We define a sequence _S_ of _t_ values to use for sampling, such as a strided schedule like ¯ _S_ schedule= (1 _,_ 3 _α_ ¯ _,S_ 5 _t, ..., T_ , which can be used to obtain corresponding sampling variances _−_ 1). Given the training noise schedule _αt_ , we can obtain the sampling noise

We can compute _p_ ( _xSt−_ 1 _|xSt_ ) as _N_ ( _µθ_ ( _xSt, St_ ) _,_ Σ _θ_ ( _xSt, St_ )). Note that Σ _θ_ ( _xSt, St_ ) is parameterized as a range between _βSt_ and _β_[˜] _St_ so it will automatically be rescaled for the shorter diffusion process.

To evaluate sample quality for reduced numbers of sampling steps, we use a stride _K_ over timesteps to reduce the total number of sampling steps from _T_ to _T/K_ . In Figures 5a and 5c, we evaluate FIDs for an _L_ hybrid model and an _L_ simple model that were trained with 4000 diffusion steps, using 30, 50, 100, 150, 200, 400, and 4000 sampling steps. We do this for multiple checkpoints throughout training. We find that the _L_ simple model suffers much more in sample quality when using a reduced number of sampling steps, whereas our _L_ hybrid model maintains sample quality. Furthermore, we find that using more sampling steps becomes increasingly beneficial throughout training. However, 100 sampling steps is still sufficient to achieve near-optimal FIDs for our fully trained models.

In initial experiments, we found that although constant striding did not significantly affect FID, it drastically reduced log-likelihood. To address this, we use a strided subset of timesteps as for FID (with stride _K_ ), but we also include every _t_ from 1 to _T/K_ . This requires _T/K_ extra evaluation steps, but greatly improves log-likelihood compared to the uniformly strided schedule. In Figures 5b and 5d we present log-likelihoods with this modified strided schedule.

## 5 SCALING MODEL SIZE

In the previous sections, we showed algorithmic changes that improved log-likelihood and FID without changing the amount of training compute. However, a trend in modern machine learning is that larger models and more training time tend to improve model performance (Kaplan et al., 2020;

(2020) results in a larger increase.

Chen et al., 2020a; Brown et al., 2020). Given this observation, we investigate how FID and NLL scale as a function of model size. Our results suggest that diffusion models can achieve better and better performance as training compute increases.

To measure how performance scales with compute, we train four different models on ImageNet 64 _×_ 64 with the _L_ hybrid objective described in Section 3.1. To change model capacity, we apply a depth multiplier across all layers, such that the first layer has either 64, 96, 128, or 192 channels. Note that our previous experiments used 128 channels in the first layer. Since the depth of each layer affects the scale of the initial weights, we scale the Adam learning rate for each model by 1 _/[√]_ channel multiplier, such that the 128 channel model has a learning rate of 0.0001 (as in our other experiments).

Figure 6a and 6b show how FID and NLL improve relative to compute. These plots reveal that, to achieve optimal performance for a given amount of compute, it often makes sense to train a larger model for fewer iterations, rather than training a smaller model to convergence. We note that these models do not achieve optimal log-likelihoods because they were trained with our _L_ hybrid objective and not directly with _L_ vlb to keep both good log-likelihoods and sample quality. The x-axis in both figures is the theoretical amount of training compute, assuming full hardware utilization.

## 6 RELATED WORK

Chen et al. (2020b) and Kong et al. (2020) are two recent works that use diffusion models to produce high fidelity audio conditioned on mel-spectrograms. Concurrent to our work, Chen et al. (2020b) use a combination of improved schedule and _L_ 1 loss to allow sampling with fewer steps with very little reduction in sample quality. However, compared to our unconditional image generation task, their generative task has a strong input conditioning signal provided by the mel-spectrograms, and we hypothesize that this makes it easier to sample with fewer diffusion steps.

Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. (2020) explored score matching in the image domain, and constructed an adversarial training objective to produce better _x_ 0 predictions. However, they found that choosing a better network architecture removed the need for this adversarial objective, suggesting that the adversarial objective is not necessary for powerful generative modeling.

## 7 CONCLUSION

We have shown that, with a few modifications, diffusion models can sample much faster and achieve better log-likelihoods with little impact on sample quality. Here we summarize our main findings:
- Our cosine noise schedule improves NLL (and sometimes FID) compared to the linear schedule from Ho et al. (2020).

- Learning Σ _θ_ using our parameterization and _L_ hybrid objective provides a good trade-off between NLL and FID. More importantly, it allows sampling with many fewer steps without decreased sample quality.
- One can optimize _L_ vlb directly using our importance sampling technique to achieve the best possible NLL at the expense of sample quality.

The combination of these results makes diffusion models an attractive choice for generative modeling, since they combine good log-likelihoods, high-quality samples, and fast sampling with a wellgrounded, stationary training objective. Furthermore, we have investigated how diffusion models scale with the amount of available training compute, and found that more training compute trivially leads to better sample quality and log-likelihood. These results indicate that diffusion models are a promising direction for future research, especially as the affordability of compute increases over time.

9

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper presents well-motivated improvements to denoising diffusion probabilistic models, specifically addressing the gap in log-likelihood performance and sampling efficiency. The proposed learned variance parameterization and cosine noise schedule are technically sound and empirically validated. The derivation of the importance sampling for the VLB objective is a highlight, effectively mitigating gradient noise issues. However, there are minor technical ambiguities regarding the precise indexing of noise schedules during strided sampling and some OCR-induced notation errors in the variance definitions.

comments.title: OCR error in variance interpolation formula
comments.explanation: Equation 15 defines the learned variance as an interpolation between $\beta_t$ and $\tilde{\beta}_t$. However, Figure 1a and the surrounding text indicate that $\beta_t > \tilde{\beta}_t$. In standard diffusion literature (and as implied by the authors' later description of $\beta_t$ as the 'large' variance extreme), the interpolation usually assigns $v$ to the upper bound and $(1-v)$ to the lower bound, or vice-versa. While mathematically a convex combination, the provided OCR text $\Sigma_{\theta}(x_t, t) = \exp(v \log \beta_t + (1-v) \log \tilde{\beta}_t)$ effectively means $\Sigma = \beta_t^v \cdot \tilde{\beta}_t^{(1-v)}$. This is a valid interpolation, but the reader should ensure $v$ is bounded (e.g., via sigmoid) or the model is stable, which the text mentions was checked 'in practice'. Given the OCR context, the formula is likely missing a sigmoid over $v$, but the intent is clear.

comments.title: Ambiguity in strided sampling index calculation
comments.explanation: In Section 4, the authors describe how to sample with fewer steps by defining a sequence $S$. The formula provided in the text for $\bar{\alpha}_{S_t}$ (underneath Equation 19) is circular or potentially mis-indexed in the text description. While the intent is to define a new noise schedule $\beta'_{t}$ for the sub-sampled process such that the marginal distributions $q(x_{S_t} | x_0)$ remain consistent with the original process, the notation $\bar{\alpha}_{S_t} = (\bar{\alpha}_{S_t} / \bar{\alpha}_{S_{t-1}})$ is likely a typo for the transition variance calculation $\beta'_{t} = 1 - \bar{\alpha}_{S_t} / \bar{\alpha}_{S_{t-1}}$. A reader attempting to implement this exactly as written might be confused by the identity-like expression.

comments.title: Potential sign error/offset in cosine schedule definition
comments.explanation: In Equation 17, the cosine schedule is defined to maintain variance information longer. However, the standard requirements for $\bar{\alpha}_t$ are that it starts at 1 (at $t=0$) and ends near 0 (at $t=T$). Checking the formula: at $t=T$, the argument is $((1+s)/(1+s)) \cdot \pi/2 = \pi/2$, and $\cos^2(\pi/2) = 0$. At $t=0$, the argument is $(s/(1+s)) \cdot \pi/2$. For $\bar{\alpha}_0$ to be 1, the authors normalize by $f(0)$ in Equation 18. While this is mathematically consistent, the choice of $s=0.008$ (implied by the bin size discussion) means $f(0)$ is very close to 1. The derivation is sound, but the text lacks a explicit mention that $\bar{\alpha}_T$ is clipped to avoid the singularity in $\beta_t = 1 - \bar{\alpha}_t / \bar{\alpha}_{t-1}$ at the very last step, which most practitioners would need to implement.

comments.title: Missing details on stop-gradient necessity
comments.quote: we also apply a stop-gradient to the $\mu_{\theta}(x_t, t)$ output for the $L_{vlb}$ term.
comments.explanation: This is a key methodological choice to ensure $L_{simple}$ remains the primary driver for the mean. However, the authors do not specify if this stop-gradient is applied only to the mean or also to the input $x_t$ within the $L_{vlb}$ calculation. If the network for $\Sigma$ and $\mu$ shares many parameters (as is standard in U-Nets), the gradient from $L_{vlb}$ still reaches the shared backbone via the variance branch. The justification is 'to prevent $L_{vlb}$ from overwhelming $L_{simple}$', but without the stop-gradient, the mean might move towards a value that optimizes local KL divergence rather than the global sample quality. This is an important detail for reproducibility that is slightly underspecified regarding the exact architecture split.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a well-structured and compelling set of improvements to denoising diffusion probabilistic models, demonstrating significant gains in log-likelihood and sampling efficiency. The hybrid objective, cosine noise schedule, and importance sampling technique are all well-motivated and supported by strong empirical results. The ablation studies are thorough, and the scaling analysis provides valuable insights into the behavior of diffusion models under increased compute. Despite minor presentation issues and one moderate technical concern regarding the definition of the hybrid objective, the contributions are substantial and the conclusions are largely supported by the evidence. The paper advances the state of the field and is likely to influence future work.

comments.title: Hybrid objective omits explicit dependence on Σ_θ in L_simple
comments.quote:
Since _L_ simple doesn’t depend on Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ), we define a new hybrid objective: 

$$ L_{\text{hybrid}} = (1 - \lambda) L_{\text{simple}} + \lambda L_{\text{vlb}} $$
comments.explanation: The text states that L_simple does not depend on Σ_θ, which is correct under the standard formulation where only the mean µ_θ is predicted and used in the loss. However, when learning Σ_θ, the sampling distribution p_θ(x_{t-1}|x_t) depends on it, yet L_simple as originally defined (as a reweighted MSE on ϵ) still does not involve Σ_θ. The hybrid objective combines L_simple (ignoring Σ_θ) with L_vlb (which does depend on Σ_θ), but the paper does not clarify how gradients are passed to the variance prediction head within L_simple. The stop-gradient on µ_θ for the L_vlb term is mentioned, but not how the network learns Σ_θ from L_simple. This creates ambiguity: is Σ_θ only trained via L_vlb in the hybrid objective? If so, the objective is correct as stated, but the text should clarify that Σ_θ receives no signal from L_simple. As written, a reader might assume L_simple has been modified to include a likelihood term, which it has not.

comments.title: Inconsistency in βt definition from α̅_t
comments.quote: To go from this definition to variances _βt_ , we note that _βt_ = 1 _− α_ ¯ _αt_ ¯ _−t_ 1
comments.explanation: The equation as OCR-rendered reads βt = 1 − α̅_t / α̅_{t−1}, but standard diffusion formulations define β̃_t = 1 − α̅_t / α̅_{t−1}, and α_t = 1 − βt, so α̅_t = ∏_{s=1}^t α_s. Therefore the correct expression for βt in terms of α̅ is βt = 1 − α̅_t / α̅_{t−1}, assuming α̅_0 = 1. While this appears correct in intent, the OCR output renders it as 'α_ ¯ −t 1', which could be misread as α̅_{−t−1} or similar. Given surrounding context and Figure 3b, which shows α̅_t decreasing monotonically, the intended meaning is almost certainly βt = 1 − α̅_t / α̅_{t−1}. This is a known identity in diffusion models, so the issue is likely OCR corruption rather than author error. Still, in the absence of LaTeX rendering, a reader might misparse the subscript.

comments.title: Missing justification for stop-gradient on µθ in L_vlb
comments.quote: we also apply a stop-gradient to the _µθ_ ( _xt, t_ ) output for the _L_ vlb term. This way, _L_ vlb can guide Σ _θ_ ( _xt, t_ ) while _L_ simple is still the main source of influence over _µθ_ ( _xt, t_ ).
comments.explanation: The authors apply a stop-gradient to µθ in the L_vlb term to prevent interference with the learning signal from L_simple, which is empirically stronger for sample quality. This is a reasonable design choice, but the paper provides no justification for why this doesn't harm the optimization of Σ_θ, which depends on the full L_vlb term. Since L_vlb contains a KL divergence that couples µθ and Σ_θ, freezing µθ could lead to a mismatch between the assumed mean and the learned variance. A standard approach would be to use the current µθ but stop its gradient, which the authors likely do, but they should clarify that this is a form of decoupled optimization and acknowledge that it modifies the true VLB gradient. The concern is resolved by context (it works empirically), but the omission weakens the theoretical justification.

comments.title: Ambiguity in importance sampling proposal distribution
comments.quote:
we employ importance sampling: 

$$ L_{\text{vlb}}^{\text{resampled}} = \mathbb{E}_{t \sim p_t} \left[ \frac{1}{p_t} L_t \right] $$

Since _E_ [ _L_[2] _t_[]][is][unknown][beforehand][and][may][change][throughout][training,][we][maintain][a][history] of the previous 10 values for each loss term, and update this dynamically during training.
comments.explanation: The formula shows importance sampling with proposal distribution p_t, but the text does not specify how p_t is constructed from the loss history. A reader might reasonably infer that p_t ∝ |L_t| or p_t ∝ Var(L_t), but the most effective scheme would be p_t ∝ ||∇_θ L_t||^2 or similar gradient-based measure, not just the loss magnitude. The paper mentions maintaining a history of 'values for each loss term' but doesn’t define what is stored (raw L_t? gradient norm? moving average?). Given that the goal is to reduce gradient noise, the proposal should target high-gradient-variance terms. The method works empirically, but the underspecification makes reproduction difficult. This rises to moderate concern because importance sampling schemes can be highly sensitive to the choice of proposal.

comments.title: Parameter inconsistency in cosine schedule design
comments.quote: We chose to use _cos_[2] in particular because it is a common mathematical function with the shape we were looking for.
comments.explanation: The paper refers to using 'cos^2' in the cosine schedule (β_t ∝ sin^2(...) is standard), but earlier defines α̅_t = f(t/T)^2 where f is cosine-shaped. The function is not explicitly defined, but Figure 3b suggests α̅_t ≈ cos^2(π/2 * t/T). The term '_cos_[2]' appears to mean 'cos squared' rather than 'cos to the power 2' or 'cos with subscript 2'. This is a minor OCR issue that doesn't affect understanding—readers familiar with the follow-up literature know this refers to the squared cosine schedule—but in isolation, it could be ambiguous. However, the surrounding explanation and visual in Figure 3b resolve the ambiguity, so the issue is minor.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents significant improvements to denoising diffusion probabilistic models, achieving competitive log-likelihoods while maintaining high sample quality. The authors introduce several valuable modifications: a learned variance parameterization, a cosine noise schedule, and importance sampling for training. The experiments are thorough and the results convincing. However, there are some mathematical expressions that appear to have OCR errors or formatting issues that could confuse readers. The most significant issues are in the precise definitions of the cosine schedule and the importance sampling weights.

comments.title: Cosine schedule definition unclear
comments.quote: To go from this definition to variances βt, we note that βt = 1 − ¯αt¯αt−1[1].[In practice, we clip][βt][to be] no larger than 0.999 to prevent singularities at the end of the diffusion process near t = T.
comments.explanation: The equation for the cosine schedule appears to have formatting issues. The authors define ¯α_t using a cosine function, but the OCR seems to have corrupted the mathematical expression. The relationship β_t = 1 - ¯α_t/¯α_{t-1} is correct in principle, but the formatting makes it hard to parse. This is a key definition for their main contribution, so clarity is important. While the concept of a cosine schedule is sound, readers may struggle to reproduce the exact schedule used.

comments.title: Importance sampling weight equation garbled
comments.quote: Since E[L[2]t[]][is][unknown][beforehand][and][may][change][throughout][training,][we][maintain][a] history of the previous 10 values for each loss term, and update this dynamically during training.
comments.explanation: The equation for the importance sampling weights appears to have OCR errors. The authors mention weighting timesteps proportionally to the square root of the expected loss magnitude, but the mathematical expression is corrupted. This makes it difficult for readers to understand exactly how the importance sampling is implemented. The concept itself (weighting by loss magnitude) is sound and well-justified by the gradient noise analysis, but the precise formulation needs to be clearer.

comments.title: Strided sampling equation unclear
comments.quote: Given the training noise schedule αt, we can obtain the sampling noise ¯αSt[−] 1). Given the training noise schedule αt, we can obtain the sampling noise
comments.explanation: The equation for computing ¯α_{S_t} when using fewer sampling steps has formatting issues. The concept of using a subset of timesteps for faster sampling is clear and well-motivated, but the mathematical relationship between the training schedule and the sampling schedule is hard to decipher due to OCR errors. This could make it challenging for readers to implement the accelerated sampling method.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper provides a significant and technically sound advancement in the field of Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs). The authors successfully identify practical limitations of the original DDPM formulation, specifically regarding log-likelihood performance and sampling speed. The proposed solutions—a parameterization of the variance to allow learning, a new noise schedule (cosine), and importance sampling for the VLB objective—are well-motivated and empirically validated. The experimental results, including comparisons on ImageNet and CIFAR-10, are clear and convincing. The primary issues identified relate to minor notations and definitions that are technically underspecified or slightly inconsistent, which, while unlikely to hinder a practitioner attempting to implement the methods, present minor lapses in formal rigor.

comments.title: Inconsistency in variance indexing
comments.quote:
it is also possible to sample using an arbitrary set of t values. We define a sequence S of t values to use for sampling... Given the training noise schedule α ¯ t, we can obtain the sampling noise
βSt = 1 - α ¯ St / α ¯ St-1
comments.explanation: In Section 4, the authors define the sampling variance for a subset of steps S. The formula provided is $\beta_{St} = 1 - \bar{\alpha}_{St} / \bar{\alpha}_{St-1}$. However, based on the standard DDPM definition (Ho et al. 2020), this should likely be $\beta_{St} = 1 - \bar{\alpha}_{St} / \bar{\alpha}_{S_{t-1}}$, where $S_{t-1}$ is the previous element in the sequence S, not the index $St-1$ itself. While the intent is clear (the ratio of the cumulative products $\bar{\alpha}$ at successive sampling steps determines the variance), the notation is prone to misinterpretation as a standard index subtraction.

comments.title: Implicit definition of beta schedule
comments.quote: To go from this definition to variances βt, we note that βt = 1 - α ¯ αt ¯ -t 1.
comments.explanation: The OCR rendering 'βt = 1 - α ¯ αt ¯ -t 1' is garbled. Based on standard literature, the intended formula for $\beta_t$ in terms of $\bar{\alpha}$ is $\beta_t = 1 - (\bar{\alpha}_t / \bar{\alpha}_{t-1})$. While the context of the cosine schedule makes the goal clear, the definition itself is mathematically mangled due to the OCR process and potentially flawed in the original notation. A reader relying solely on this sentence to implement the schedule would be confused.

comments.title: Missing definition of importance sampling probability
comments.quote:
we employ importance sampling:
L= E t ~ p(t) [ (1/2p(t)) || epsilon - epsilon_theta ||^2 ]
comments.explanation: The paper introduces importance sampling to optimize $L_{vlb}$, but it never explicitly defines the distribution $p(t)$ used for importance sampling. It mentions maintaining a history of loss values, but does not state the functional form of $p(t)$ (e.g., whether it is proportional to the mean absolute loss or another metric). This is an underspecified methodological detail that is critical for reproduction.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents a well-motivated and empirically solid set of improvements to Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs), particularly the learned variance parameterization, cosine noise schedule, and importance-sampled VLB training. The ablation studies and scaling analysis are thorough, and the claims about sample quality vs. log-likelihood trade-offs are generally backed by the reported results. However, there are a few areas where methodological descriptions are too vague for precise reproduction, and the framing of certain claims in the abstract/introduction slightly overstates findings that are actually dependent on the training budget. Addressing the underspecified importance sampling procedure, clarifying the strided sampling protocol, and tempering the hybrid vs. direct VLB comparison would strengthen the paper.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a significant advancement in the field of denoising diffusion probabilistic models, clearly demonstrating improved log-likelihoods and practical sampling speeds through concrete algorithmic changes (hybrid objective, cosine schedule, importance sampling). The technical approach is sound, and the empirical results are persuasive. However, there are two areas that require clarification to ensure reproducibility and accuracy in reporting. First, the description of the importance sampling implementation is mathematically underspecified, as it is unclear how the weights are derived from the 'history of previous 10 values'. Second, there is an inconsistency in the labeling of comparison baselines between Table 1 and Table 3, where the same log-likelihood value (3.77) is attributed to both a configuration called 'Improved' and 'Diffusion (Ho et al.)'. Addressing these issues will strengthen the paper's rigor.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents valuable improvements to diffusion models for log-likelihood and sampling efficiency. The core ideas (learning the variance, cosine noise schedule, importance sampling for the VLB) are sound and well-motivated. However, the manuscript suffers from a critical omission of the variance parameterization equation (Section 3.1), an ambiguous description of the strided sampling schedule for log-likelihood (Section 4), and a minor overclaim in the abstract about the hybrid objective's superiority over direct likelihood optimization. These issues, especially the missing equation, hinder reproducibility and clarity.

comments.title: Missing equation for variance parameterization
comments.quote: In particular, our model outputs a vector _v_ containing one component per dimension, and we turn this output into variances as follows:
comments.explanation: The paper states that the model outputs a vector v which is transformed into variances, but the actual transformation formula is missing from the text. Without this equation, the method for learning Σ_θ(x_t,t) is underspecified, making it impossible to reproduce this key contribution. The surrounding text mentions interpolation between β_t and β̃_t in the log domain, but the exact operation (e.g., log interpolation, softmax-like combination) is not given. This is a major omission for a novel parameterization.

comments.title: Ambiguous strided schedule for log-likelihood evaluation
comments.quote: To address this, we use a strided subset of timesteps as for FID (with stride K), but we also include every _t_ from 1 to _T/K_.
comments.explanation: The description of the evaluation schedule for log-likelihood with reduced steps is unclear. For FID, a strided subset (stride K) yields T/K steps. The phrase 'also include every t from 1 to T/K' suggests that the first T/K steps are taken consecutively (no stride), but it is then ambiguous how the remaining steps are handled. This could be interpreted as taking the first T/K steps directly and then striding over the rest, which would yield a total number of steps > T/K. The exact definition needed for reproducibility is missing.

comments.title: Overclaim about hybrid objective versus direct likelihood optimization
comments.quote: We find surprisingly that, with our hybrid objective, our models obtain better log-likelihoods than those obtained by optimizing the log-likelihood directly
comments.explanation: This claim appears in the abstract and introduction. While it holds when comparing L_hybrid to L_vlb with uniform timestep sampling (as shown in Figure 4a and Table 1, 200K iterations), the paper later introduces an importance-sampled L_vlb that outperforms L_hybrid (Table 1, 200K and 1.5M rows). The statement is therefore not universally true and could mislead a reader who does not read the full paper. It should be qualified to specify that the comparison is against optimizing L_vlb with uniform sampling.
